text:
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST, DYNAMIC SUSCEPTIBILITY CONTRAST PERFUSION IMAGING (DSC-PWI), AND MULTIVOXEL MR SPECTROSCOPY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 64-year-old female with known metastatic non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma (EGFR L858R-mutant). History of stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to right frontal lobe metastasis (20 Gy in 1 fraction) and right cerebellar hemisphere metastasis (22 Gy in 1 fraction) approximately 11-12 months prior. Recent surveillance MRI demonstrated enlargement of the treated right frontal lesion from 0.6 cm to 1.4 cm with new surrounding signal abnormality. Dedicated advanced MRI sequences obtained to distinguish radiation necrosis from viable tumor recurrence prior to planned management decisions.

COMPARISON: Prior MRI brain with gadolinium (approximately 4 weeks prior, at which time the right frontal lesion measured 1.4 cm with perilesional T2/FLAIR signal). Original post-SRS follow-up MRI (approximately 10 weeks after SRS, showing right frontal lesion at 0.6 cm with decrease from the pre-treatment size of 1.1 cm).

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar multisequence MRI of the brain performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner. Sequences include: sagittal T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial DWI (b=0, 1000), axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial T1 pre-contrast, dynamic susceptibility contrast perfusion imaging using gadolinium bolus tracking, post-contrast axial and coronal T1-weighted images (MPRAGE), and multivoxel 2D chemical shift imaging (2D-CSI) MR spectroscopy centered on the right frontal lesion and a symmetric contralateral reference region. Intravenous gadolinium contrast administered without complication.

FINDINGS:

Right Frontal Lobe (Primary Site of Interest):
There is an irregularly marginated area of predominantly ring-like and nodular enhancement in the right frontal lobe (white matter, posterior to the precentral gyrus), currently measuring approximately 1.4 x 1.2 cm in axial dimensions (compared to 0.6 x 0.5 cm on the most recent prior MRI approximately 4 weeks ago, and compared to 1.1 x 0.9 cm at initial presentation prior to SRS). There is surrounding T2/FLAIR hyperintensity extending over a roughly 3.5 cm zone, considerably more prominent than on the prior study, consistent with worsening vasogenic edema and/or treatment effect involving the adjacent white matter. Mild local mass effect on the right frontal horn of the lateral ventricle is noted; no significant midline shift. No restricted diffusion within the lesion to suggest acute ischemic change. SWI demonstrates a focus of susceptibility artifact at the periphery of the lesion, likely reflecting hemosiderin or prior blood products related to treatment. There is no new hemorrhage.

DSC Perfusion Imaging -- Right Frontal Lesion:
Relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) mapping demonstrates focally elevated rCBV within the enhancing nodular component of the right frontal lesion, with an rCBV ratio of approximately 2.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter (threshold for concern for viable tumor generally accepted at rCBV ratio >2.0-2.5 at this institution). The rCBV elevation is most pronounced in the posterior and inferior portion of the ring-enhancement, corresponding to the region of most prominent nodular enhancement on post-contrast T1 images. A small area of relatively lower rCBV is also present within the central portion of the lesion, which may reflect a necrotic core. Overall, the perfusion pattern is not typical of pure radiation necrosis (which classically demonstrates reduced rCBV) and is more consistent with a viable, hypervascular tumor component.

MR Spectroscopy -- Right Frontal Lesion:
Multivoxel 2D-CSI MR spectroscopy was performed with voxel placement encompassing the enhancing lesion and surrounding perilesional tissue, with a reference voxel placed in the contralateral left frontal white matter. Within the voxel most closely overlapping the enhancing nodular component:
- Choline (Cho) peak is markedly elevated
- N-acetylaspartate (NAA) peak is markedly reduced
- Choline:NAA (Cho:NAA) ratio is elevated at approximately 2.6 (normal <1.0; ratio >2.0 highly suspicious for viable neoplasm at this institution)
- Creatine (Cr) peak is moderately reduced
- A small lipid/lactate peak is present, which can be seen in both necrosis and high-grade tumor
- The Cho:Cr ratio within the lesion is elevated compared to the contralateral reference voxel
In the perilesional voxels, there is a moderate reduction in NAA with less prominent choline elevation, consistent with treatment effect and edema in the adjacent white matter.
The spectroscopic profile, taken in conjunction with the perfusion findings, is most consistent with viable tumor recurrence rather than pure radiation necrosis. A mixed picture (i.e., tumor admixed with some treatment-related change) cannot be entirely excluded.

Right Cerebellar Hemisphere (Previously Treated Lesion):
The previously identified and SRS-treated right cerebellar hemisphere metastasis is no longer visualized as a discrete enhancing lesion. There is a small focal area of T2 signal change measuring approximately 0.3 cm in greatest dimension without enhancement, likely representing residual gliotic change or radiation-related change at the prior treatment site. No recurrence identified.

Remainder of the Brain:
No new enhancing lesions are identified on the current study. No additional parenchymal metastases. No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest carcinomatous meningitis. The cerebral sulci and gyral pattern appear grossly within normal limits for patient age without evidence of global leptomeningeal disease. Ventricles are otherwise normal in caliber. No hydrocephalus. Brainstem and posterior fossa structures appear intact outside the previously described cerebellar treatment site change. Sella and skull base appear unremarkable. The major dural venous sinuses appear patent on post-contrast imaging.

Calvarial Structures and Orbits:
No aggressive osseous lesion identified within the calvarium. Orbits are grossly intact.

IMPRESSION:

1. Enlarging right frontal enhancing lesion (1.4 x 1.2 cm, increased from 0.6 x 0.5 cm on prior study), now accompanied by substantially increased surrounding vasogenic edema, at the site of prior stereotactic radiosurgery. The combination of elevated rCBV on DSC perfusion imaging (rCBV ratio approximately 2.8) and a markedly elevated choline:NAA ratio on MR spectroscopy (approximately 2.6) is most in keeping with viable tumor recurrence/regrowth rather than radiation necrosis alone. A mixed tumor/necrosis picture cannot be definitively excluded without tissue confirmation.

2. Previously treated right cerebellar hemisphere lesion: no residual enhancing disease; small focal T2 signal abnormality at prior SRS site consistent with treatment sequela.

3. No new intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement.

4. Mild local mass effect from right frontal lesion and surrounding edema on the right lateral ventricle; no midline shift.

Findings discussed with the referring oncology team at the time of interpretation.
